key_insight: The paper reframes PINN–FEM coupling as a Robin–Neumann domain-decomposition problem and shows that a mode-matched interface impedance stabilizes the exchange while accounting for PINN training error.
engineering_relevance: This enables meshless fluid or surrogate subdomains to couple robustly to FEM models in moving-boundary and contact problems without remeshing, which is valuable for FSI and multiphysics simulation.
method: Model each solver as a Steklov–Poincar�e9 trace-to-flux operator at the interface.
Replace unstable Dirichlet–Neumann coupling with Robin–Neumann coupling using a closed-form interface impedance.
Use a Fourier-mode interface probe to estimate the PINN’s resolvable interface eigenvalues and diagnose spectral limits.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports contraction predictions within 7% on 1D and 2D Poisson tests, eigenvalue recovery within 0.5%, and a Stokes/contact example where the reaction matches submerged weight to 0.4% under mesh refinement.
watch_item: The warm-started PINN can drift off the Stokes manifold over long horizons, so long-time stability and spectral under-resolution remain practical concerns.
paper_title: Robin-Neumann Coupling of PINN and FEM Solvers: A Steklov-Poincaré View, with Application to Fluid-Structure Interaction with Contact
